county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from Oneida County. Site of Franklin, Idaho's first permanent white settlement (1860). Strong LDS settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County Recorder in Preston has deed records from 1913. Franklin (the town) was established in 1860 by Mormon colonists from Cache Valley, Utah, making it the first permanent Euro-American settlement in Idaho — settlers initially believed they were still in Utah Territory. LDS ward records from the 1860s forward are the primary genealogical source and are extensively indexed on FamilySearch. Franklin Stake records document virtually every pioneer family. The Bear River Massacre (1863) of Shoshone people occurred near the county; military records at NARA and the Idaho State Historical Society document this event. Check Oneida County for pre-1913 records. Preston and its surrounding communities (Weston, Dayton, Whitney) have distinct LDS ward records. The Franklin County Historical Society has pioneer family files. Sugar beet farming and dairying shaped the 20th-century economy.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1913 to present. County Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Bingham County. Eastern Idaho county adjacent to Yellowstone National Park. Strong LDS settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fremont County Recorder in St. Anthony has deed records from 1893. Fremont County borders Yellowstone National Park — Yellowstone's western entrance attracted guides, outfitters, and hospitality workers whose records are at NARA and the Yellowstone Heritage and Research Center. LDS ward records are primary sources for many Fremont County families, with wards organized in the 1880s-1890s; records are indexed on FamilySearch. The Fremont County Historical Society museum in St. Anthony has pioneer family files. St. Anthony was an early LDS settlement center; the Upper Snake River Valley Historical Society preserves regional records. Island Park, Ashton, and Warm River communities have distinct settlement histories tied to railroading and logging. Check Bingham County for pre-1893 records. The Teton Dam collapse (1976) affected Fremont County families; disaster relief records document displaced residents.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1915 from parts of Boise and Canyon counties. Western Idaho agricultural county in the Payette River valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gem County Recorder in Emmett has deed records from 1915. Emmett is in the Payette River valley, which was irrigated for agriculture in the early 1900s. The Emmett Valley was originally a gold mining area in the 1860s before transitioning to farming. Cherry orchards became famous; the Emmett Cherry Festival records document agricultural families. Check Boise and Canyon counties for pre-1915 records. The Gem County Historical Society museum in Emmett has pioneer family files and photographs. BLM's GLO records document homestead entries in the valley. The Emmett Messenger-Index newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS and provide excellent coverage of local families. Some LDS families settled in the valley; ward records on FamilySearch supplement county records. Logging in the surrounding mountains generated Forest Service records at NARA Seattle.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from Lincoln County. South-central Idaho agricultural county on the Snake River Plain.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gooding County Recorder in Gooding has deed records from 1913. The county was named for Frank R. Gooding, Idaho governor and sheep rancher. Irrigation from the Milner Dam and the Twin Falls Land and Water Company transformed the Snake River Plain into farmland; land company records at ISHS document purchasers and settlers. The Idaho School for the Deaf and Blind (established 1906) in Gooding has student records (restricted access). Check Lincoln County for pre-1913 records. Basque sheepherders worked the ranges surrounding the irrigated farms; Basque boarding house and sheep company records survive at ISHS and the Basque Museum in Boise. The Gooding County Historical Society has local family files. The Hagerman Fossil Beds National Monument area attracted early settlers; homestead records at BLM. Japanese-American farm families were present before WWII internment.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Idaho's largest county by area. Includes the Frank Church River of No Return Wilderness.
county_metadata.research_tips: Idaho County Recorder in Grangeville holds deed records from 1864, among the oldest in the state. Idaho County is the largest county by area in Idaho and one of the largest in the continental US. The county was the site of major gold discoveries at Florence (1861) and Warren (1862), predating the county's formal creation — mining district records at ISHS document miners including a significant Chinese community at Warren. The Nez Perce War of 1877 occurred largely in Idaho County; military records at NARA and the Nez Perce National Historical Park document battles at White Bird Canyon and the Clearwater. The Bicentennial Historical Museum in Grangeville has pioneer family files. The Nez Perce Reservation partially overlaps the county. Elk City, Dixie, and other remote mining camps generated records scattered across multiple repositories. The Camas Prairie farming community around Grangeville has distinct agricultural records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from Fremont County. Eastern Idaho agricultural county. Strong LDS settlement. Birthplace of television inventor Philo Farnsworth.
county_metadata.research_tips: Jefferson County Recorder in Rigby has deed records from 1913. Rigby is famous as the hometown of Philo T. Farnsworth, inventor of electronic television. LDS ward records are the primary genealogical source for Jefferson County — multiple wards were organized in the 1890s-1900s, and records are extensively indexed on FamilySearch. The Farnsworth TV and Pioneer Museum in Rigby has local family files. Irrigation from the Great Feeder Canal and other Snake River projects brought farmers; Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA Denver document water rights holders. Check Fremont County for pre-1913 records. Roberts and Menan communities have distinct LDS ward records. Mud Lake and the Camas Creek area attracted homesteaders in the early 1900s; BLM's GLO records document land patents. Sugar beet farming employed seasonal laborers from diverse backgrounds.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Lincoln County. South-central Idaho agricultural county. Near the Minidoka War Relocation Center (WWII Japanese-American internment).
county_metadata.research_tips: Jerome County Recorder in Jerome has deed records from 1919. Jerome County was developed through the North Side Irrigation Project, which opened thousands of acres to farming in the early 1900s — irrigation company and land sale records at ISHS document original purchasers. The Minidoka War Relocation Center (Hunt Camp) was located near Jerome/Eden; NARA holds extensive internment camp records including family registration forms, housing records, and employment files for over 9,000 Japanese Americans. Check Lincoln County for pre-1919 records. The Jerome County Historical Society museum has pioneer family files and irrigation-era photographs. Magic Valley dairy farming attracted European immigrant families in the mid-1900s. The Jerome North Side News newspaper archives are on microfilm at ISHS. The Twin Falls-Jerome Intercounty Bridge connected these agricultural communities.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 as one of the original Idaho Territory counties. Northern Idaho county. Contains Coeur d'Alene, the Panhandle's largest city.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kootenai County Recorder in Coeur d'Alene holds deed records from 1864, making it one of the oldest record sets in Idaho. Coeur d'Alene grew as a steamboat and railroad town serving the Coeur d'Alene mining district to the east (Shoshone County). The Coeur d'Alene Tribe historically occupied the area; the Coeur d'Alene Indian Reservation borders the county, and BIA records at NARA Seattle include census rolls and allotment records. Fort Sherman (originally Fort Coeur d'Alene, 1878-1900) military records at NARA document soldiers and their families. The Museum of North Idaho in Coeur d'Alene has local genealogy collections and steamboat-era records. Timber was a major industry; Rutledge Timber Company, Ohio Match Company, and other lumber company records at the University of Idaho Library identify workers. Rathdrum, Post Falls, and Hayden Lake communities have distinct settlement histories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1888 from Nez Perce County. Home to the University of Idaho (1889). Palouse farming region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Latah County Recorder in Moscow has deed records from 1888. Moscow is home to the University of Idaho (founded 1889, Idaho's land-grant university) — university archives and alumni records provide genealogical data. The Latah County Historical Society in Moscow has one of Idaho's strongest local genealogy collections, including family files, oral histories, and photograph collections. The Palouse region's rich farmland attracted Scandinavian, German, and other immigrant families. Check Nez Perce County for pre-1888 records. The McConnell Mansion museum preserves Victorian-era Moscow history. Juliaetta, Kendrick, and Troy were farming and milling communities with distinct settlement histories. The University of Idaho Library Special Collections holds timber industry records, mining company records, and Idaho political papers. The Moscow-Pullman Daily News newspaper archives extend to the 1890s. The Palouse Patit Creek area attracted early settlers before the university era.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1869 from Idaho County. Central Idaho mountain county. Lewis and Clark passed through Lemhi Pass (1805). Includes the birthplace of Sacagawea.
county_metadata.research_tips: Lemhi County Recorder in Salmon holds deed records from 1869, making it one of Idaho's oldest county record sets. Lemhi County encompasses some of the most remote territory in the lower 48 states. Lewis and Clark crossed Lemhi Pass in 1805, and Sacagawea's birthplace near present-day Salmon is commemorated. The Lemhi Shoshone people were removed from the Lemhi Valley to Fort Hall Reservation in 1907; BIA removal records at NARA Denver document affected families. Gold mining at Leesburg (1866) and other camps generated mining district records at ISHS. The Lemhi County Historical Museum in Salmon has pioneer family files and mining-era records. Salmon River rafting and outfitting attracted guides whose permits are documented in Forest Service records. Ranching in the Lemhi and Pahsimeroi valleys is documented in brand records at ISHS. Check Idaho County for pre-1869 records.
